nodes.name: Project Type
nodes.desc: Primary functional use of the constructed facility — the single most impactful attribute for benchmarking. A $50M hospital and a $50M warehouse have completely different cost profiles, risk patterns, and trade compositions. Custom values are common across companies — normalization to a standard taxonomy is critical for cross-company comparison.

nodes.name: Project Subtype
nodes.desc: Further classification within primary project type — customer-defined refinement. Examples: 'Class A Office', 'Acute Care', 'Fulfillment Center', 'Garden-Style Apartments', 'Charter School'. No industry standard taxonomy for subtypes — each company defines their own.

nodes.name: Sector
nodes.desc: Public vs. private funding and ownership — determines procurement rules, bonding requirements, prevailing wage, and compliance burden. Public projects have transparency requirements and standardized procurement. Private projects move faster with fewer constraints. P3 (Public-Private Partnership) is a growing hybrid model.

nodes.name: Work Type
nodes.desc: New construction vs. renovation vs. addition — fundamentally different risk and cost profiles. Renovation projects have 2-3x the change order rate of new construction due to unknown existing conditions. Mixing work types in benchmarks produces misleading results. Must be filtered before any cost or schedule comparison.

nodes.name: Program Type
nodes.desc: Owner's capital program classification — groups projects by investment purpose across a portfolio. Useful for owners running rollout or prototype programs (retail chains, QSR, healthcare clinics) where standardized scope enables tight benchmarking. Customer-defined with no industry standard.

nodes.name: Estimated Value
nodes.desc: Owner's initial budget or estimated total project cost at authorization — the 'what we thought it would cost' baseline. Set before contracts are awarded. Different from contract value (which is the GC agreement). Used to measure cost growth from initial estimate through final completion.

nodes.name: Contract Value
nodes.desc: Contracted amount between owner and GC/CM — the GMP, lump sum, or negotiated target. This is the number subcontractors and the market see. Updated via change orders over the project lifecycle. Represents the GC's contractual obligation.

nodes.name: Total Value Band
nodes.desc: Bucketed range for benchmarking by project scale — log-scale bands work better than linear because construction costs, risks, and outcomes don't scale linearly. A $100M project is not simply 10x a $10M project — it's a fundamentally different kind of project.

nodes.name: Cost per SF
nodes.desc: Total project cost divided by gross building area — the most comparable unit cost metric across building projects. Only meaningful for building projects (not infrastructure). Varies enormously by type: warehouse $100-200/SF vs. hospital $600-1500/SF. Must normalize by type and region.

nodes.name: Hard Cost
nodes.desc: Direct construction cost excluding soft costs (design fees, permits, FF&E, land, financing). Typically 60-80% of total project cost. Isolates construction cost from owner's total investment. Essential for apples-to-apples cost benchmarking.

nodes.name: Contingency Budget
nodes.desc: Owner and/or GC contingency allowance at project start — the risk reserve. Owner contingency covers scope changes; GC contingency covers construction risks. How fast contingency burns is a leading indicator of project risk. Separate tracking of owner vs. GC contingency is best practice.

nodes.name: Final Cost at Completion
nodes.desc: Actual total cost when project is complete — includes all change orders, allowance adjustments, and final settlements. The gap between contract value and final cost is the most important financial performance indicator. Only available after project completion.

nodes.name: Address
nodes.desc: Street address of the project site — source for all derived geographic attributes. Geocoding converts address to latitude/longitude for distance, climate zone, and cost index calculations. Not directly used in benchmarks but foundational for derived attributes.

nodes.name: City
nodes.desc: City or municipality where the project is located — enables local market analysis. Labor rates, material costs, and subcontractor availability vary significantly by metro area. City-level benchmarks useful when enough project volume exists within a metro.

nodes.name: State / Province
nodes.desc: State or province jurisdiction — major benchmarking dimension. State drives labor law (prevailing wage, right-to-work), building code edition, licensing requirements, and tax structure. Top 20 US states cover 80%+ of commercial construction volume.

nodes.name: Country
nodes.desc: Country where the project is located — relevant for international benchmarking. Most construction data models are US-centric. International projects require currency normalization and different code/standard references.

nodes.name: ZIP / Postal Code
nodes.desc: Postal code of the project site — enables micro-market analysis and cost index application. RS Means and other cost databases use ZIP code for location cost factors. ZIP-level granularity useful for labor market and subcontractor availability analysis.

nodes.name: Climate Zone
nodes.desc: ASHRAE or IECC climate zone based on project location — drives envelope design, energy code compliance, and expected weather delay days. Zone 1 (hot-humid) to Zone 8 (subarctic) have fundamentally different design and construction requirements. Critical for HVAC and enclosure benchmarking.

nodes.name: Seismic Design Category
nodes.desc: ASCE 7 seismic classification based on site soil conditions and proximity to faults. Higher SDC = more expensive structure and connections. Significant cost impact in SDC D and above — affects structural design, fire protection (seismic bracing), and MEP (flexible connections).

nodes.name: Region
nodes.desc: Geographic region for market-level analysis — aggregates state-level data for regional trends. Region definitions vary by organization but should align with labor market and cost index boundaries. Typically 8-10 regions sufficient for US benchmarking.

nodes.name: Planned Start Date
nodes.desc: Scheduled construction start date — typically Notice to Proceed (NTP) or mobilization date. The contractual baseline against which all schedule performance is measured. Some owners define this as first day of site work; others use NTP date.

nodes.name: Actual Start Date
nodes.desc: Actual date construction work began on site — may differ from planned start due to permitting, financing, or design delays. The gap between planned and actual start captures pre-construction schedule risk.

nodes.name: Planned Completion Date
nodes.desc: Contractual substantial completion date — when the owner can occupy the facility. Different from final completion (all punch items done, all contracts closed). Most contracts use substantial completion as the contractual milestone. Liquidated damages typically attach to this date.

nodes.name: Actual Completion Date
nodes.desc: Actual date of substantial completion — only available when the project reaches that milestone. The gap between contractual and actual completion is the core schedule performance metric. Average schedule growth is 10-20% for well-managed projects.

nodes.name: Planned Duration
nodes.desc: Contractual construction duration in calendar days from NTP to substantial completion. Industry typically uses calendar days for overall duration (not working days). Excludes pre-construction. Duration is strongly correlated with project type and value band.

nodes.name: Actual Duration
nodes.desc: Actual construction duration from start to substantial completion — compare to planned duration for schedule performance, compare to benchmarks by type/size for relative performance.

nodes.name: Preconstruction Duration
nodes.desc: Time from design authorization to NTP — often ignored but significantly affects construction outcomes. Design-build and CMAR compress this phase. DBB has the longest preconstruction. Longer preconstruction correlates with more complete documents and fewer field changes (to a point).

nodes.name: Season of Start
nodes.desc: Season when construction begins — affects early work scope and weather delay risk. Winter starts in cold climates (ASHRAE zones 5-8) face frozen ground, concrete cold weather protection, and shorter daylight hours. Correlates with higher sitework cost.

nodes.name: Delivery Method
nodes.desc: Project delivery system defining the design-construction relationship — one of the strongest predictors of project outcomes. DBB separates design and construction (owner bears coordination risk). CMAR/GMP brings the contractor in during design (shared risk). DB puts design and construction under one contract (contractor bears risk). IPD shares risk and reward among all parties.

nodes.name: Contract Type
nodes.desc: Compensation structure between owner and GC/CM — determines who bears cost risk. GMP: GC bears overrun risk above the guaranteed maximum. Lump Sum: GC bears all cost risk. Cost Plus: owner bears cost risk. Contract type determines who benefits from efficiency and who absorbs overruns.

nodes.name: Bid Type
nodes.desc: How the contractor was selected — affects pricing competitiveness and project relationship dynamics. Open competitive typically yields lowest initial price but highest change order rate. Negotiated/QBS (Qualifications-Based Selection) correlates with lower total cost at completion.

nodes.name: Bonding Requirement
nodes.desc: Whether performance and payment bonds are required — adds 1-3% to cost and limits the contractor pool to firms with adequate bonding capacity. Federal projects over $250K require bonds (Miller Act). Many state and local public projects have similar requirements.

nodes.name: Prevailing Wage
nodes.desc: Whether prevailing wage requirements apply — adds 10-30% to labor cost depending on market differential between prevailing and market rates. Federal (Davis-Bacon) and state prevailing wage laws apply to public projects. Some private projects trigger prevailing wage through public incentives or tax credits.

nodes.name: Union / Open Shop
nodes.desc: Labor agreement type on the project — affects labor rates, productivity assumptions, and trade jurisdiction rules. Union projects have higher hourly rates but potentially different productivity. Project Labor Agreements (PLAs) common on large public projects. Trade jurisdiction rules affect crew composition and flexibility.

nodes.name: Gross Building Area
nodes.desc: Total enclosed floor area including all floors — the primary size dimension for cost benchmarking. Gross area includes occupied, mechanical, circulation, and wall space. Different from rentable or usable area. Most important physical attribute for cost-per-SF calculations.

nodes.name: Number of Stories Above Grade
nodes.desc: Count of floors above ground level — affects structural system, vertical transportation requirements, fire protection, and egress. High-rise (typically 7+ stories or 75+ ft) triggers different code requirements for structure, egress, and fire protection. Each additional floor adds elevator, stair, and structural cost premium.

nodes.name: Number of Stories Below Grade
nodes.desc: Count of floors below ground level (basement levels) — below-grade construction costs 1.5-3x above-grade per SF due to excavation, shoring, waterproofing, and dewatering. Parking structures below grade are common in urban settings.

nodes.name: Building Height
nodes.desc: Height from grade to highest occupied floor or roof peak — triggers high-rise code requirements (typically 75+ ft). Determines crane type (tower vs. mobile), wind load design, and elevator requirements.

nodes.name: Site Area
nodes.desc: Total area of the project site — affects logistics, staging, and site improvement cost. Tight urban sites increase logistics cost due to limited staging, laydown, and equipment access. Large sites increase paving, utilities, and landscaping scope.

nodes.name: Structural System
nodes.desc: Primary structural framing system — the single biggest cost and schedule driver for any building. Steel frame: faster erection, lighter foundation. Concrete frame: potentially cheaper for regular grids, longer cycle time. Mass timber: emerging for mid-rise, different fire protection. Choice affects cost, schedule, trade composition, and crane requirements.

nodes.name: Foundation Type
nodes.desc: Primary foundation system — driven by geotechnical conditions, not project type. Deep foundations (driven piles, drilled shafts) add $20-100/SF to substructure cost vs. shallow foundations (spread footings). Foundation type has significant schedule and cost impact in early phases.

nodes.name: Number of Units
nodes.desc: Count of individual units — apartments, hotel rooms, patient rooms, beds, or seats. Per-unit cost is the primary benchmark for residential, hospitality, and healthcare. Only applicable for certain building types. Normalizes cost comparison across different building sizes within the same type.

nodes.name: Parking Type
nodes.desc: Type of parking provided — parking structure can be 15-25% of total project cost. Underground parking is most expensive ($50-80K/space). Structured above-grade: $20-40K/space. Surface: $2-5K/space. Parking type significantly affects total project cost and should be tracked for cost benchmarking.

nodes.name: Construction Type
nodes.desc: IBC construction type classification — determines allowable building height and area, required fire-resistance ratings for structural elements, and material restrictions. Type I-A (most fire resistant, most expensive) to Type V-B (least fire resistant, least expensive). Directly drives structural material and fireproofing cost.

nodes.name: Occupancy Group
nodes.desc: IBC occupancy classification — drives code requirements for occupant load, egress, fire protection, accessibility, and special inspections. I-2 (hospitals) is the most demanding occupancy. Occupancy group determines fire sprinkler requirements, exit width, and structural importance factor.

nodes.name: Building Code Edition
nodes.desc: Edition of the building code adopted by the jurisdiction — affects energy, structural, and accessibility requirements. Jurisdictions adopt different editions on different timelines. Newer codes have stricter energy requirements. Some jurisdictions have significant local amendments.

nodes.name: Fire Sprinkler Requirement
nodes.desc: Whether automatic fire sprinklers are required or provided — sprinkler cost is $3-8/SF but allows construction type trade-offs (increased height and area under IBC). Almost all new commercial and multi-family construction is now fully sprinklered. NFPA 13 for commercial, NFPA 13R for residential.

nodes.name: Accessibility Standard
nodes.desc: Applicable accessibility standard — affects interior layout, restroom count, fixture selection, and door hardware. ADA applies to all commercial and public buildings. Fair Housing Act applies to multi-family (4+ units). State codes often exceed federal ADA requirements.

nodes.name: LEED Certification
nodes.desc: Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design — the most widely recognized green building certification system. Point-based system across categories: Sustainable Sites, Water Efficiency, Energy & Atmosphere, Materials & Resources, Indoor Environmental Quality, Innovation. Certification levels: Certified (40–49 pts), Silver (50–59), Gold (60–79), Platinum (80+). LEED requirements affect material selection, construction waste management, commissioning scope, and documentation.

nodes.name: WELL Certification
nodes.desc: WELL Building Standard certification level — focuses on occupant health and wellness (air quality, water quality, light, fitness, comfort). Growing adoption in corporate office and multi-family. Adds operational requirements beyond construction. Newer standard than LEED with different focus areas.

nodes.name: Energy Code
nodes.desc: Energy code or standard the project must meet — becoming a bigger cost driver as standards tighten. Energy code determines insulation R-values, glazing performance, HVAC efficiency, and lighting power density. Net Zero and Passive House add significant premium (10-25%+).

nodes.name: Net Zero Target
nodes.desc: Whether the project targets net-zero energy consumption — requires on-site renewable energy (typically solar), high-performance envelope, and ultra-efficient MEP systems. Adds 10-25% to construction cost. Growing requirement from institutional owners and ESG-driven developers.

nodes.name: Resilience Requirements
nodes.desc: Special requirements for disaster resilience — essential facilities (hospitals, fire stations, EOCs) must remain operational post-disaster. Requires enhanced structural, MEP, and envelope systems beyond standard code. Flood, hurricane, seismic, blast, and storm shelter requirements each add distinct cost and design requirements.

nodes.name: Owner Type
nodes.desc: Type of entity that owns the project — drives procurement method, bonding requirements, and decision-making speed. Private developers prioritize speed and cost. Public owners have procurement rules and transparency requirements. Institutional owners (universities, hospitals) have long approval chains but take a long-term perspective.

nodes.name: GC / CM Firm
nodes.desc: General contractor or construction manager firm name — enables contractor-level performance benchmarking across projects. With enough data: compare change order rates, schedule performance, safety records, and cost outcomes by contractor.

nodes.name: Architect of Record
nodes.desc: Architect responsible for design and construction administration — A/E firm performance significantly affects construction outcomes. Firms with higher document quality produce fewer RFIs and change orders. Architect responsiveness during CA affects submittal turnaround.

nodes.name: Number of Subcontractors
nodes.desc: Total count of subcontractor firms on the project — a proxy for project complexity. More subs = more coordination overhead, more meetings, more potential conflicts. A 100-sub project is fundamentally different from a 20-sub project.

nodes.name: Peak Workforce
nodes.desc: Maximum number of workers on site during peak construction — a labor intensity metric. Peak workforce / SF indicates labor density. High peak on a constrained site creates safety and logistics challenges. Peak timing typically aligns with MEP rough-in or interior finishes.

nodes.name: Self-Performed Work
nodes.desc: Percentage of work self-performed by the GC (not subcontracted) — affects risk profile and cost structure. Most GCs self-perform 5-15% (general conditions, concrete, carpentry). Trade-specific GCs may self-perform 40-60%. Higher self-perform = more direct labor risk but potentially higher margin.

nodes.name: New vs. Existing
nodes.desc: Whether the project involves existing structures or is entirely new — renovation content fundamentally changes cost and risk. Existing conditions discovery is the #1 source of changes on renovation projects. Asbestos, lead paint, structural deficiency, and hidden utilities are common unknowns. Must be filtered before benchmarking.

nodes.name: Occupied During Construction
nodes.desc: Whether the building or adjacent spaces are occupied during construction work — adds logistics constraints, safety requirements, work hour restrictions, noise/vibration limits, and security requirements. Adds 10-25% to schedule and significant cost for temporary partitions, phased shutdowns, and after-hours work.

nodes.name: Phased Construction
nodes.desc: Whether the project is built in distinct phases with partial occupancy or turnover between phases. Each phase requires its own punch list, inspections, and turnover. Temporary barriers, life safety systems, and egress must be maintained between phases. Adds remobilization cost and schedule complexity.

nodes.name: Fast-Track
nodes.desc: Whether design and construction overlap — construction starts on early packages (sitework, foundations) while later packages (MEP, interiors) are still being designed. Compresses overall schedule by 15-30% but increases RFI volume and change order rate due to incomplete design information.

nodes.name: Site Constraints
nodes.desc: Notable constraints on the construction site — multiple constraints compound and fundamentally change project complexity. An urban constrained site with adjacent occupied buildings and environmental contamination is a different project than a greenfield suburban site. Site constraints drive logistics cost and methodology decisions.

nodes.name: BIM Requirement
nodes.desc: Level of Building Information Modeling required on the project — BIM adoption level correlates with coordination quality and clash detection effectiveness. LOD 300-350 is becoming standard for commercial projects above $25M. LOD 400 for complex MEP. Digital twin for owner-operators who want lifecycle data.

nodes.name: Modular / Prefab Content
nodes.desc: Percentage of construction using off-site prefabrication or modular methods — shifts labor from field to factory. Reduces on-site workforce peak but requires earlier procurement and more detailed coordination. Factory-controlled environment typically produces higher quality. Growing trend in healthcare (bathroom pods) and hospitality (modular rooms).

nodes.name: Specialty Systems
nodes.desc: Nurse call, intercom, paging, distributed antenna (DAS), and clock systems. Project-type dependent — nurse call for healthcare, DAS for large buildings. Trades: low-voltage technicians.

nodes.name: Project Status
nodes.desc: Current state of the project in its lifecycle — determines what data is available and how the project should be treated in benchmarks. Only closed/archived projects have final cost and schedule data. Active projects have in-progress metrics. Cancelled projects should be excluded from benchmarks.

nodes.name: Current Phase
nodes.desc: Current construction phase based on the phases taxonomy — enables phase-aware analytics and 'where should you be at this point' benchmarks. A project in MEP Rough-In should have a certain percentage of changes processed, RFIs closed, and submittals approved. Links to taxonomy-phases.csv.

nodes.name: Percent Complete
nodes.desc: Overall project completion percentage — multiple methods: cost-based (earned value), milestone-based, or units-based. Cost-based is most common for overall project. The gap between percent complete and percent schedule elapsed indicates schedule health.

nodes.name: Earned Value — CPI
nodes.desc: Cost Performance Index — the most reliable predictor of final cost performance. Calculation: BCWP (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) / ACWP (Actual Cost of Work Performed). CPI < 1.0 means over budget. CPI at 20% complete is the strongest predictor of final cost outcome — it rarely recovers.

nodes.name: Earned Value — SPI
nodes.desc: Schedule Performance Index — measures schedule efficiency. Calculation: BCWP (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) / BCWS (Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled). SPI < 1.0 means behind schedule. Less reliable than CPI because schedule recovery is more achievable than cost recovery through acceleration and resequencing.

nodes.name: Cost Growth
nodes.desc: Percentage increase from contract value to final cost at completion — the most important financial performance metric in construction. Calculation: (Final Cost − Contract Value) / Contract Value × 100. Average is 4-8% for well-managed projects, 15-25% for complex renovations. Source entities: contract value, final cost at completion.

nodes.name: Schedule Growth
nodes.desc: Percentage increase from planned to actual duration — the core schedule performance metric. Calculation: (Actual Duration − Planned Duration) / Planned Duration × 100. Average is 10-20% for well-managed projects. Renovation and fast-track projects have higher schedule growth.

nodes.name: RFI Density
nodes.desc: Number of RFIs per $1M of contract value — measures document quality and design completeness. High RFI density correlates with incomplete documents, aggressive fast-track schedule, or complex design. Calculation: Total RFI Count / (Contract Value / $1M). Source entities: RFIs, contract value.

nodes.name: Safety Incident Rate (TRIR)
nodes.desc: Total Recordable Incident Rate — OSHA-standard safety metric. Calculation: (Total Recordable Incidents × 200,000) / Total Labor Hours Worked. Industry average is 2.5-3.5 for commercial construction. Lower is better. Leading indicator when tracked alongside observation frequency.

nodes.name: Submittal Approval Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of submittals approved on first submission — measures specification clarity, subcontractor preparation quality, and A/E review efficiency. Calculation: First-Submission Approvals / Total Submittals × 100. Re-submittals compound schedule delay.

nodes.name: Project Health Score
nodes.desc: Composite metric that aggregates signals across financial, schedule, quality, and safety domains into a single project health indicator. Financial inputs: budget variance (actual vs. planned), change order rate, contingency burn rate. Schedule inputs: critical path float erosion, milestone slip frequency, schedule update consistency. Quality inputs: observation closure rate, inspection pass rate, punch item density trend. Safety inputs: observation frequency, incident rate, hazard recurrence. Each dimension scored 0-100 using percentile ranking against projects of similar type and size. Weighted composite provides at-a-glance project status for portfolio-level monitoring.

nodes.name: Administrative Burden
nodes.desc: The cumulative time and effort spent on paperwork, data entry, compliance documentation, and process overhead instead of productive construction work. Driven by: number of active workflows, submittal volume, RFI volume, daily log requirements, lien waiver tracking, payment application cycles, and cross-tool data re-entry. Every manual handoff between systems adds administrative drag. Projects with high admin burden have slower response times, more missed deadlines, and lower team morale. Reducing admin burden is the #1 reason field teams resist adopting new tools — if it adds work instead of removing it, they won't use it.

nodes.name: Decision Speed
nodes.desc: How fast the project team can identify an issue, gather the information needed, get the right people involved, and make a decision that unblocks work. Driven by: RFI response time, submittal cycle time, approval chain length, change event turnaround, and meeting cadence. Every day a decision is delayed costs money — crews waiting, equipment idling, schedule slipping. The information-to-action pipeline is the critical path for most decisions: field discovers issue → RFI created → design team responds → change event processed → approval chain clears → work resumes. Each handoff is a potential bottleneck.

nodes.name: Margin Protection
nodes.desc: The ability to maintain planned profit margin from bid through project completion. Margin erodes through: untracked scope creep, missed change order opportunities, cost overruns on self-performed work, subcontractor disputes, retainage timing, and slow payment collection. The typical GC operates on 3-8% net margin — a single missed change event or cost coding error can wipe out an entire project's profit. Margin protection requires real-time cost visibility, proactive change management, and tight budget-to-actual tracking across every cost code.

nodes.name: Information Gaps
nodes.desc: Missing, stale, or inaccessible information that prevents the project team from making timely decisions. Sources: incomplete design documents (generating RFIs), missing submittal data (delaying procurement), outdated schedules (misaligning trades), inconsistent cost reports (hiding budget problems), and siloed field data (preventing early warning). Information gaps compound — a missing spec section generates an RFI, which delays a submittal, which delays procurement, which delays installation, which triggers a schedule claim. Closing information gaps before they cascade is the highest-leverage intervention in construction.

nodes.name: Risk Reduction
nodes.desc: The ability to identify, quantify, and mitigate risks before they become costly problems. Key risk surfaces: safety incidents, quality defects, schedule delays, cost overruns, subcontractor default, regulatory non-compliance, and weather exposure. Leading indicators — observation frequency, near-miss reports, inspection pass rates, RFI velocity, float erosion — can predict problems 2-6 weeks before they hit. Projects that track and act on leading indicators have 30-50% fewer surprises at completion. Risk reduction is not about eliminating risk — it's about seeing it early enough to respond.

nodes.name: Profit Protection
nodes.desc: Preserving and recovering the profit built into the original estimate through disciplined cost tracking, change management, and claim documentation. Profit leaks through: unbilled extra work, unprocessed owner change orders, cost code misallocation hiding overruns, retainage cash flow gaps, and failure to back-charge subcontractors for defective work. Every dollar of revenue recognized without corresponding cost control is phantom profit. Profit protection requires connecting the estimating basis to field execution to final cost — a continuous chain from bid to closeout.
